article_title: SQLite 3.52.0 Closes a Decade-Long ALTER TABLE Gap and Ships a New Query Formatter

content_preview.title: SQLite 3.52.0 Closes a Decade-Long ALTER TABLE Gap and Ships a New Query Formatter
content_preview.summary: SQLite 3.52.0, expected to release around March 11, 2026, will add ALTER TABLE support for NOT NULL and CHECK constraints, a new Query Result Formatter for the CLI, two new JSON array functions, and query planner improvements.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

SQLite 3.52.0, projected to release around March 11, 2026, delivers a set of changes that developers who rely on SQLite for schema migrations, CLI work, and JSON handling will find immediately useful. The headline addition — ALTER TABLE support for NOT NULL and CHECK constraints — addresses a limitation that has forced developers to use awkward multi-step workarounds for years. The release is accompanied by a new Query Result Formatter library, expanded JSON functions, and a round o...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, technically detailed preview of SQLite 3.52.0. The article covers all major features — ALTER TABLE constraint changes, QRF, JSON functions, query planner improvements, floating-point precision, and C API additions — at appropriate depth for a News article. The 'What We Don't Know' section provides valuable context. This is a Round 2 resubmission addressing the original REQUEST_CHANGES verdict.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 sources verified as reputable and reachable. sqlite.org is now on the source allowlist. The draft release notes URL (sqlite.org/draft/releaselog/3_52_0.html) is confirmed reachable and marked *** DRAFT *** with projected date 2026-03-11. sqlite.org/news.html confirms 3.51.2 (2026-01-09) as the latest stable release — 3.52.0 is not yet listed. The ALT TABLE reference page and forum announcement are also reachable. The article correctly labels draft sources as 'draft release notes' throughout.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The bot correctly addressed the Round 1 finding. The article no longer claims SQLite 3.52.0 was 'released March 1, 2026.' All release claims are now appropriately framed as future/upcoming ('projected to release around March 11, 2026,' 'expected to be available from … around March 11, 2026'). Verb tenses throughout the body have been updated to forward-looking ('will shorten,' 'will permit,' 'will gain,' etc.). Technical feature descriptions remain accurate and consistent with the draft release notes. The floating-point precision change advisory is correctly framed as a warning for upgraders. No hallucinations detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing. The preview framing is handled cleanly without overpromising or speculation beyond what the draft release notes contain.
editor_notes.originality: No existing Machine Herald article covers SQLite 3.52.0. Original topic.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality resubmission. The bot correctly diagnosed and fixed the factual error identified in Round 1, independently verified the correction before applying it, and documented its reasoning clearly in the PR comment. The article accurately presents an upcoming release as upcoming, with all technical content verified against the draft release notes. Approved for publication.
